title: Lemon drizzle cake
subtitle: The British loaf cake with a sharp, soaking drizzle
excerpt: A 900 g loaf tin cake made by the all-in-one method, soaked while still warm with lemon juice and sugar. The drizzle sets to a thin crust on top and keeps the crumb moist for days.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: lemon-flavoured pound cakes with a sugar glaze appear in the 1880s edition. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: lemon-scented plain cakes, including cakes soaked with a wine-sugar syrup (the direct precursor to the lemon drizzle). The drizzle-soaking technique as applied to a lemon loaf is a twentieth-century British household development; it is documented in interwar newspaper baking columns as a way of keeping a plain cake moist.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 loaf cake (900 g / 2 lb tin, cuts into 8 slices)
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the whole un-drizzled cake, wrapped in cling film, for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature, then make a fresh drizzle before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well for 3 days in a tin. The crumb actually improves on day two.

recipeTools.notes: 900 g / 2 lb tin, greased and lined.
The batter is forgiving; a wooden spoon works.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of fresh lemon juice and caster or granulated sugar, spooned over a warm loaf cake straight from the oven. The warm crumb absorbs the liquid; the sugar dries on the surface to a thin, crunchy crust.

content.text: The lemon drizzle loaf is a one-bowl cake: all the batter ingredients go in together and are beaten briefly. What makes it different from a plain pound cake is the finish. The drizzle syrup goes on while the cake is still hot from the oven, soaking into the crumb and drying to a thin, crackly sugar crust on the surface. The cake keeps well for several days, growing slightly stickier under the crust as it sits.

attrs.title: Unwaxed lemons
attrs.body: Buy unwaxed lemons for this recipe — the wax coating on standard lemons is edible but dulls the zest. The zest carries most of the lemon flavour in the batter; the juice goes into the drizzle.

items.prepNote: unwaxed; zest only for the batter
juice for the drizzle (from the same lemons)

content.text: The drizzle uses granulated rather than caster sugar; the coarser crystals dry to a slightly crunchier crust. Caster sugar works too, but the texture is softer.
Prepare the tin and heat the oven

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin and line the base and long sides with a strip of baking paper, leaving an overhang on each side to use as a handle when lifting the cake out.

Weigh {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter, {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar, {{eggs}} eggs (at room temperature), {{self-raising-flour}} sifted self-raising flour, and {{baking-powder}} baking powder into the large bowl. Add the finely grated zest of both lemons. Beat with a hand mixer on medium for 2 minutes until smooth, pale, and well combined. This is the all-in-one method: everything in together, beaten just enough to emulsify. Don't beat for longer than 2 minutes or the cake will toughen.

Scrape the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Bake for 48 to 52 minutes; the top should be a deep golden brown, the edges will have pulled slightly from the sides of the tin, and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. A slight crack along the top is normal and correct; the crumb is tightly bound in a loaf tin and needs to split to rise.
While the cake bakes, stir the juice of both lemons together with {{granulated-sugar}} granulated sugar. Don't heat it; the

should be a loose slurry, not a dissolved syrup. When the cake comes out of the oven, immediately prick the top all over with a skewer at 1 cm intervals and spoon the drizzle evenly over the warm surface. Leave the cake in the tin for 15 minutes while the drizzle soaks in and the sugar crust sets.
Use the baking paper overhangs to lift the cake out of the tin onto a rack. Leave to cool completely before slicing. The crust is fragile while warm.

attrs.heading: When things go sideways
attrs.intro: The three most common problems with lemon drizzle.

items.cause: Cake slightly over-baked, or the drizzle went on too late.
items.fix: Pull the cake the moment the skewer comes out clean. Apply the drizzle within 2 minutes of the cake leaving the oven; a warm crumb absorbs it; a cool one doesn't.

items.symptom: Soggy band at the top under the crust
items.cause: Too much drizzle applied too fast, pooling rather than soaking.
items.fix: Apply the drizzle in three batches, waiting 30 seconds between each so the crumb has time to absorb it.

items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or the batter was beaten for too long (knocking the air out after the flour went in).
items.fix: Confirm the oven temperature with a thermometer. Beat just until smooth; 2 minutes is enough.

content.text: An orange and poppy seed drizzle cake follows the same recipe with the zest and juice of two oranges and 2 tablespoons of poppy seeds folded into the batter. A lime version is sharper and works well with a tablespoon of desiccated coconut in the batter.
